_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2 + display math + LA trim + apps + extra exercises
title: Lump sum vs. annuity
deck: One deposit, or many. Both grow with compound interest, but the formulas look different because of how the deposits stack up over time.
objectives: Use the lump-sum and annuity future-value formulas to project savings forward across long time horizons.

content.text: Imagine setting aside $200 every month for thirty years inside a retirement account that earns 6% per year, compounded monthly. Over those three decades, the total dollar amount of contributions is just twelve months times thirty years times $200, which works out to $72,000. The actual balance at the end of year thirty, however, sits at about $200,903.

The extra $128,903 was never deposited. It is interest, earned by the formula on top of the contributions that were made. That gap, between what we put in and what ends up in the account, is the central magic of long-horizon saving, and it is what makes a steady stream of modest deposits a more powerful tool than most non-savers realize. This section gives the two formulas that describe both of the most common deposit patterns, a single lump sum and a regular series of equal payments, and shows when to reach for each one.

A lump sum is a single deposit made today and left alone to compound. We met its formula in Section 3.3: A = P(1 + r/n)nt, the same compound-interest formula with the natural reading “ today's money, tomorrow's value.” An annuity is a different shape entirely: equal deposits made at regular intervals (monthly, quarterly, annually) over a long horizon. Every deposit gets to compound from the moment it is made; later deposits get less time to grow than earlier ones.

content.caption: Figure 3.5. Thirty years of $200 monthly contributions at 6%. The deposits total $72,000; the final balance is $200,903. The $128,903 gap is compound interest on the stream of contributions.

content.text:
Definition 3.5. The future value of a lump sum uses the compound-interest formula directly:
A = P (1 + r/n)n t
The future value of an annuity with periodic payment PMT, deposited n times per year for t years at annual rate r, is
A = PMT · [(1 + r/n)n t − 1] / [r/n]
The lump-sum formula compounds one deposit forward; the annuity formula adds up the future value of every deposit made along the way.

Margin noteThe annuity formula in this section is an ordinary annuity, which assumes each deposit is made at the end of the compounding period. An annuity due assumes deposits at the beginning and is worth slightly more (by a factor of 1 + r/n) because each deposit gets one extra period of compounding. Most 401(k) and savings contracts approximate ordinary annuities.
3.5.2  Why time is the biggest lever

The three knobs on the annuity formula are the deposit amount PMT, the rate r, and the horizon t. Doubling the deposit doubles the future value, linearly. Doubling the rate roughly doubles the interest portion. Doubling the time, however, more than doubles the future value, because compounding is exponential in t. The most underrated savings advice in personal finance is to start early; the math really does favor the long horizon.

Common mistakeConfusing contributions with final balance. The contributions are what we deposited, summed up; the final balance is what those deposits grew into. A retirement account showing $200,000 with only $72,000 in lifetime contributions is doing exactly what it is supposed to. The difference is the interest the formula earned for us.

Example 3.5. $500 a month for twenty years at 6%.

A new graduate sets up an automatic contribution of $500 into an index fund every month, earns an average return of 6% per year (compounded monthly), and leaves it alone for twenty years. We want the future value of the account and the dollar amount that came from interest rather than contributions.

Solution.

Step 1. Name the pieces. PMT = $500 per month, r = 0.06 per year, n = 12 deposits per year, t = 20 years.

Step 2. Plug into the annuity formula.

A = 500 · [(1 + 0.06/12)12 · 20 − 1] / [0.06/12]

Step 3. Compute the numerator. The monthly rate is 0.005, and 240 periods of growth give

(1.005)240 ≈ 3.31020
(1.005)240 − 1 ≈ 2.31020

Step 4. Divide by the per-period rate and multiply by PMT.

A ≈ 500 · (2.31020) / (0.005) ≈ 500 · 462.04 ≈ $231,020

Step 5. Separate contributions from interest. Contributions total 500 × 12 × 20 = $120,000, so interest earned is about $231,020 − $120,000 = $111,020.

The account ends up at roughly $231,000, nearly twice the dollar amount of contributions. The same monthly deposit, held for thirty years instead of twenty, would have grown to around $502,000, with about $322,000 in interest. The extra decade alone almost triples the interest earned.
The biggest lever on the future value is not the rate. It is the number of years.

In the wildRetirement accounts (401(k), Roth IRA, Traditional IRA), college 529 plans, and savings clubs all use the annuity future-value formula behind the scenes. The Social Security trust fund itself is a giant annuity calculation. Excel's built-in FV() function accepts the same four arguments we used here, and most modern retirement calculators are thin wrappers around exactly this formula.

content.problem: You invest **$200/month** in a retirement account earning **7% APR compounded monthly** for **40 years**. What is the future value?

A 40-year-old has **$50,000 already saved** for retirement and is contributing **$0/month** (just letting it ride). At 7% annual return, compounded annually, what is the value at age **65** (25 years later)?

content.text:
1. What is the future value of a lump-sum deposit of $5,000 left for 15 years at 5% compounded annually? Round to the nearest cent.

    Show answer

    Answer. About $10,394.64. A = 5,000(1.05)15 &approx 10,394.64.

  2. What is the future value of an annuity that contributes $100 a month for 10 years at 6% per year, compounded monthly? Round to the nearest cent.

    Show answer

    Answer. About $16,387.93. A = 100 · [(1.005)120 − 1] / 0.005 &approx 16,387.93.

  3. For the annuity in the previous problem, how much of the final balance is interest, and how much is contributions?

    Show answer

    Answer. Contributions are 100 × 12 × 10 = $12,000 interest is about 16,387.93 − 12,000 = $4,387.93.

  4. Two graduates each plan to save $300 a month at 7% per year. Graduate A saves for 30 years; Graduate B saves for 40 years. By roughly what factor does B's account exceed A's?

    1. About 1.3 times
    2. About 2 times
    3. About 4 times
    4. About the same; the extra decade barely matters
    Show answer

    Answer. B. A's balance is about $366,000 B's balance is about $787,000. The extra ten years roughly doubles the account, even though contributions only increased by 33%. The exponential effect of compounding rewards time generously.

  5. Which formula is correct for the future value of an annuity?

    1. A = PMT · n · t
    2. A = PMT · [(1 + r/n)nt − 1] / (r/n)
    3. A = PMT · (1 + r/n)nt
    4. A = PMT + r · t
    Show answer

    Answer. B. Option A ignores compounding entirely; option C is the lump-sum formula; option D has no clear meaning.

  6. Which feature of an annuity, doubled, produces the largest increase in the final balance?

    1. The monthly payment PMT
    2. The annual rate r
    3. The number of years t
    4. The number of periods per year n
    Show answer

    Answer. C. Doubling the payment doubles the balance linearly; doubling the rate roughly doubles the interest; doubling the time multiplies the balance by considerably more, because the formula's growth in t is exponential.

content.title: Future value of a lump sum
content.problem: You invest **$10,000** at **7% per year compounded annually**. How much is the lump sum worth in **25 years**?
content.solution:
**Apply A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt) with n = 1.**

A = 10,000 × (1.07)^25 ≈ 10,000 × 5.4274

A ≈ **$54,274**

A single $10,000 left alone for 25 years at 7% grows to over $54,000 — about a 5× multiplier. The doubling period at 7% is roughly 10 years (the *Rule of 72*: 72 ÷ 7 ≈ 10.3).

content.title: Future value of an annuity (regular contributions)
content.problem: Instead of a single deposit, you save **$300/month** for **30 years** in an account paying **6% APR compounded monthly**. What is the future value?
content.solution:
**Apply the annuity formula A = M[(1 + r/n)^(nt) − 1] / (r/n).**

M = 300, r = 0.06, n = 12, t = 30, so r/n = 0.005 and nt = 360.

(1.005)^360 ≈ 6.0226

A = 300 × (6.0226 − 1) / 0.005 = 300 × 5.0226 / 0.005 = 300 × 1,004.52

A ≈ **$301,355**

Total *contributed*: $300 × 360 = $108,000. Total *earned in interest*: roughly $193,000 — almost twice what you put in. That's the headline of long-horizon investing: time + consistent contributions + compounding > lump-sum strategies for most retirement savers.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Future value formulas come in two shapes: **lump sum** for a one-time deposit, **annuity** for regular contributions. Both depend on the same inputs (rate, compounding, time), but the annuity formula adds a payment M and a sum over all periods. Retirement planning almost always uses the annuity form.

content.title: The future-value question every 25-year-old asks
content.content: "If I put **$300/month** into a Roth IRA starting at 25, what will I retire with?" This is the most common personal-finance question of the next decade, and it's a future-value-of-annuity problem. At 7% real return for 40 years, the answer is roughly **$735,000** — about $144,000 of which is your contributions, the rest (about $591,000) is compounding. The annuity formula we just typeset is what every retirement calculator on Fidelity, Vanguard, and Schwab runs in the background. When you adjust the slider "increase savings by $50/month" and watch the projection jump, that's the formula re-evaluating with M += 50.

content.text: We now own both halves of the savings toolkit: the lump-sum formula for money already in hand, and the annuity formula for money saved on a schedule.
